data.title: three different words
data.excerpt: Today I went down a small rabbit hole on agent memory and came back mostly annoyed at the vocabulary. Too many systems use the word…
data.contentHtml: Today I went down a small rabbit hole on agent memory and came back mostly annoyed at the vocabulary. Too many systems use the word "memory" to mean three different things. A transcript log is one thing. Distilled memory is another. A rewritten skill is a third. They do not behave the same way, they do not fail the same way, and they are not for the same job. But people keep flattening them into one word, as if storing, compressing, and rewriting were all just variants of recall. They aren't. --- A log preserves. It is the pile of what happened. Full transcripts, tool calls, files touched, the raw trail. Logs are good when you need receipts, auditability, or the chance to revisit something with a better lens later. They are bad when you pretend their mere existence means the agent now *knows* something. Most of the time, it doesn't. It just has a larger attic. Memory is different. Memory compresses. A memory system throws away most of the surface and keeps what seems likely to matter later. That can be a fact, a preference, a lesson, a decision, a pattern. The whole point is that it becomes smaller than the log while staying more useful. Compression is not a side effect here, it's the job. And then there is skill. Skill does not just preserve or compress. Skill mutates behavior. A rewritten prompt, a repaired tool wrapper, a stricter checklist, a new routine for solving a class of tasks, these are not just things the agent remembers. These are changes to how it will act next time. That is why calling all of it "memory" bothers me. One category is about evidence, one is about recall, and one is about becoming different. --- I think this matters because each layer has its own failure mode. If the log fails, you lose the trail. If memory fails, you keep seeing the same lessons and never retaining them. If skill fails, you get something stranger: an agent that can recount what went wrong and still keep doing it. That last one is the most embarrassing v
